text: A fifty six year old female patient was presented with a three month history of symptomatic midline incisional hernia. The past surgical history of the patient was a low anterior resection and ileostomy for distal rectal cancer in two thousand six and an ileostomy reversal operation in two thousand seven.

The patient is positioned in a hyperextension supine position. A one-centimeter transverse incision is made at the upper part of the left rectus abdominis muscle, and a balloon trocar is inserted between the left rectus abdominis muscle and the posterior rectus sheath. The space is expanded by inflating the balloon, revealing the white posterior rectus sheath below and the rectus muscle above. A second port is inserted medial to the semilunar line, and dissection of the left retro-rectus space continues until the pubis is reached.

Port III is inserted medial to the semilunar line at the umbilicus level, and the scope is switched to this port. The surgeon incises the medial aspect of the left posterior rectus sheath, reaching and opening the right posterior rectus sheath longitudinally over the falciform ligament, connecting both retro-rectus spaces. Port four is inserted under direct vision, and the camera is switched to it. The dissection of the right retro-rectus space continues towards the right semilunar line in Bogro's space, viewed from a cephalad to caudal direction.

The surgeon encounters the hernia sac and performs sharp dissection using a League Assure device, opening the sac. Multiple intra-abdominal intestinal adhesions are visible, and adhesiolysis is performed carefully without electrocautery to prevent bowel injury.

The surgeon completes blunt dissection of the right retro-rectus space, identifying the pubic bone. Dense adhesions between the previous ileostomy scar and the lateral border of the right rectus abdominis muscle at the arcuate line are sharply dissected. Peritoneal defects and posterior rectus sheaths are closed with 3-0 barbed sutures, and the linea alba is reconstructed using a running suture with 0-barbed suture.

The surgeon measures the dissected space to determine the appropriate mesh size. A 28 by 17 centimeter polypropylene mesh is inserted through the right 10 millimeter port and positioned flat between the anterior and posterior layers. The mesh is not fixed, and the flatness is controlled as the pneumoperitoneum is slowly released.
The patient was discharged uneventfully on the second post-operative day.
